source_paper_title: Order Independence in Sequential, Issue-by-Issue Voting
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Gershkov_2025, title={Order Independence in Sequential, Issue-by-Issue Voting}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0342}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0342}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Gershkov, Alex and Moldovanu, Benny and Shi, Xianwen}, year={2025}, month=aug, pages={1635–1653} }
context:
Page 23, Section 5 (end of the proof of Proposition 3, immediately before Proposition 4).

What is the equilibrium voting outcome if the chosen basis does not satisfy LAMO? We conjecture that the outcome of sequential issue-by-issue voting is then necessarily order dependent.
title: Order dependence of sequential issue-by-issue voting without LAMO bases
background_latex:
Let \(d\ge 2\) and let \((\mathbb{R}^d,\|\cdot\|)\) be a finite-dimensional normed vector space.

1) Voters and preferences. There are \(n\ge 3\) voters (with \(n\) odd). Voter \(i\) has an ideal point (peak) \(t_i\in\mathbb{R}^d\). Preferences are induced by the norm distance to the peak: voter \(i\) weakly prefers outcome \(v\) to \(v'\) iff \(\|t_i-v\|\le \|t_i-v'\|\).

2) Issues as a basis. Fix an algebraic basis \(B=\{x_1,\dots,x_d\}\) of \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Any \(v\in\mathbb{R}^d\) can be uniquely written \(v=\sum_{j=1}^d \alpha_j(v) x_j\); here \(\alpha_j(v)\in\mathbb{R}\) is the \(j\)-th coordinate of \(v\) in basis \(B\).

3) Sequential issue-by-issue voting. For a permutation \(\sigma=(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_d)\) of \(\{1,\dots,d\}\), consider the sequential procedure that determines the coordinate along \(x_{\sigma_1}\) first, then \(x_{\sigma_2}\), etc. At stage \(k\), voters report a real number for the coordinate along \(x_{\sigma_k}\); the stage outcome is the simple majority (one-dimensional) median of reported numbers. Earlier stage outcomes are observed before later-stage reports are chosen. The equilibrium concept is ex-post perfect equilibrium, and the paper focuses on the equilibrium surviving iterative elimination of weakly dominated strategies.

4) Order independence. The family of sequential procedures associated with the fixed basis \(B\) is called order independent if, for every profile \((t_1,\dots,t_n)\), the resulting equilibrium outcome in \(\mathbb{R}^d\) is the same for all permutations \(\sigma\).

5) Birkhoff--James orthogonality and LAMO. For vectors \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}^d\), \(x\) is Birkhoff--James (BJ) orthogonal to \(y\) (written \(x\dashv y\)) if \(\|x+\lambda y\|\ge \|x\|\) for all \(\lambda\in\mathbb{R}\). For a basis \(B=\{x_1,\dots,x_d\}\), let \(X_j\) be the subspace spanned by \(B\setminus\{x_j\}\). The basis satisfies left-additive mutual orthogonality (LAMO) if \(X_j\dashv x_j\) for every \(j\) (equivalently, every linear combination of the other basis vectors is BJ-orthogonal to \(x_j\)).
problem_definition_latex:
Assume a norm \(\|\cdot\|\) on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) and a basis \(B=\{x_1,\dots,x_d\}\) that does not satisfy LAMO with respect to \(\|\cdot\|\) (i.e., for some \(j\) there exists \(y\in X_j\) and \(\lambda\in\mathbb{R}\) such that \(\|y+\lambda x_j\|<\|y\|\)).

Determine whether it must follow that sequential issue-by-issue voting with respect to \(B\) is order dependent, in the sense that there exist (at least) two permutations \(\sigma,\pi\) of \(\{1,\dots,d\}\) and a profile of peaks \((t_1,\dots,t_n)\) for which the (iteratively undominated) ex-post perfect equilibrium outcomes of the corresponding sequential procedures differ in \(\mathbb{R}^d\).

location_in_paper: Page 23, Section 5 (end of the proof of Proposition 3, immediately before Proposition 4).
open_problem_quote: What is the equilibrium voting outcome if the chosen basis does not satisfy LAMO? We conjecture that the outcome of sequential issue-by-issue voting is then necessarily order dependent.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample (if the conjecture is false) would amount to specifying a norm, a non-LAMO basis, and showing order independence across permutations, which can be tested computationally by solving the induced sequential equilibrium for finite n and sampled peak profiles. However, computing equilibria under general norms can be nontrivial and may require numerical best-response and careful equilibrium selection (iteratively undominated ex-post perfect equilibrium).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects (norms and bases) can be parameterized (e.g., polyhedral norms via unit ball facets) and fitness can be based on empirically estimated order-dependence frequency or maximal outcome discrepancy across permutations over many sampled peak profiles. The main limitation is that reliably computing the relevant equilibrium under general norms may be expensive and somewhat brittle, but an evolutionary loop could still search effectively for illustrative counterexamples or supporting evidence.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
